title: Overthrow of the Usurper Magnentius.

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Magnentius in the meanwhile having made himself master of the imperial city Rome, put to death many members of the senatorial council, as well as many of the populace.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But as soon as the commanders under Constantius had collected an army of Romans, and commenced their march against him, he left Rome, and retired into the Gauls.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: There several battles were fought, sometimes to the advantage of one party, and sometimes to that of the other: but at last Magnentius having been defeated near Mursa—a fortress of Gaul—was there closely besieged.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: In this place the following remarkable incident is said to have occurred.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Magnentius desiring to reassure the courage of his soldiers who were disheartened by their late overthrow, ascended a lofty tribunal for this purpose.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: They, wishing to give utterance to the usual acclamation with which they greet emperors, contrary to their intention simultaneously all shouted the name not of Magnentius, but of Constantius Augustus.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Regarding this as an omen unfavorable to himself, Magnentius immediately withdrew from the fortress, and retreated to the remotest parts of Gaul.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Thither the generals of Constantius hastened in pursuit.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: An engagement having again taken place near Mount Seleucus, Magnentius was totally routed, and fled alone to Lyons, a city of Gaul, which is distant three days’ journey from the fortress at Mursa.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Magnentius, having reached this city, first slew his own mother; then having killed his brother also, whom he had created Cæsar, he at last committed suicide by falling on his own sword.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This happened in the sixth consulate of Constantius, and the second of Constantius Gallus, on the fifteenth day of August.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Not long after, the other brother of Magnentius, named Decentius, put an end to his own life by hanging himself.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Such was the end of the enterprises of Magnentius.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The affairs of the empire were not altogether quieted; for soon after this another usurper arose whose name was Silvanus: but the generals of Constantius speedily put him also out of the way, whilst raising disturbances in Gaul.
